title: TinyMoE on the Tiny-LM Leaderboard: A Mid-Field Result
summary: TinyMoE-100m-2x8-retrained, a sparse Mixture-of-Experts model, has been evaluated on Glint Research's Tiny-LM Leaderboard, placing 20th of 30 entries. This post reviews the architecture and the benchmark results in context.
content:
Overview

TinyMoE, shorthand for TinyMoE-100m-2x8-retrained, has been added to Glint Research's Tiny-LM Leaderboard, placing 20th out of 30 evaluated models. This post summarizes the architecture and situates the result within the surrounding field.

Architecture

TinyMoE is a sparse Mixture-of-Experts language model built on the Mixtral/Mistral architecture, intended for research and resource-constrained deployment. Rather than activating the full network for every token, it routes each token through 2 of 8 available experts per layer, which keeps inference cost low relative to its total parameter count.

Specifications:

  • Total parameters: approximately 99.8M
  • Active parameters per token: approximately 22.5M
  • Experts: 8 total, 2 active per token, across 10 layers
  • Context length: 1024 tokens
  • License: MIT

Training data was drawn from HuggingFaceTB/smollm-corpus, specifically the cosmopedia-v2 and fineweb-edu-dedup subsets. The current weights, published July 5, 2026, supersede the earlier checkpoint.

Leaderboard Position

The Tiny-LM Leaderboard evaluates models in a similar size range on WikiText-2 byte-perplexity, BLiMP, ARC-Easy, and a composite efficiency score. The relevant portion of the table is reproduced below.

RankModelOrgParamsEff.WikiText-2 byte_ppl ↓BLiMP ↑ARC-Easy ↑Training Tokens
18Byrne-86MQuazim0t086M66.152.683970.33%34.68%—
19Glint-1.3 (merged)Glint Research982K64.063.0868.7%32.5%100B
20TinyMoE-100m-2x8-retrainedFlameF0X99.8M64.032.87966.01%33.88%—
21Dumb-1.2-RC156m34.6M63.822.83864.87%34.13%210M
22Supra-Mini-v5SupraLabs7.87M63.682.7363.5%34.4%—

TinyMoE records an efficiency score of 64.03, a WikiText-2 byte-perplexity of 2.879, 66.01% on BLiMP, and 33.88% on ARC-Easy, placing it marginally ahead of Glint-1.3 and just behind Byrne-86M-Base.

Analysis

  • BLiMP performance is comparatively strong. A score of 66.01% places TinyMoE ahead of most lower-ranked entries, consistent with the expectation that MoE routing provides greater effective capacity per active parameter than a dense model of similar active size.
  • ARC-Easy performance is average. At 33.88%, TinyMoE sits in the same range as Dumb-1.2-RC1 and Unknown-2026-0628, well behind the top ten entries such as GPT-X2-125M and the Supra family.
  • Active parameter count is the more relevant comparison. With roughly 22.5M active parameters per token against 99.8M total, TinyMoE's effective compute cost is closer to the 20-30M-parameter models nearby on the board than its total parameter count would suggest. A rank of 20 under that framing is a reasonable outcome rather than a disappointing one.

Next Steps

Planned follow-up work includes additional training tokens, an assessment of whether ARC-Easy performance moves independently of BLiMP, and experimentation with expert count and routing to determine whether the gap to the top ten entries is primarily architectural or data-driven.

  • FlameF0X/TinyMoE-100m-2x8-retrained
  • Tiny-LM Leaderboard on Hugging Face Spaces

title: Evaluating Seven Models on Intel's Low-Bit Open LLM Leaderboard
summary: Seven models were submitted to Intel's Low-Bit Open LLM Leaderboard to assess performance under quantization. Results include a 4B distilled Claude model in the top 30% overall, two 1.2B LFM2.5 variants in the mid-field, and several sub-300M models scoring comparably to quantized versions of OpenAI's 20B gpt-oss model.
content:
Methodology

To obtain a standardized comparison against a broad set of open models under quantization, seven models were submitted to the Intel Low-Bit Open LLM Leaderboard. At the time of submission the leaderboard comprised 71 entries spanning roughly 150M to 30B+ parameters.

The figure above shows the submitted models (orange) alongside their nearest baselines (grey). The full rankings are summarized below.

Results Summary

Global RankModelAverageHellaSwagMMLUPiQASize (GB)
21FlameF0X/Qwen3-4B-Distilled-Claude-4.6 (NVFP4-RTN)62.6848.4666.4773.123.89
22FlameF0X/Qwen3-4B-Distilled-Claude-4.6 (MXFP4-RTN)61.1846.7164.9671.873.81
45FlameF0X/LFM2.5-1.2B-Distilled-Claude-4.6 (W4A16-RTN)46.7639.5131.9968.770.75
47FlameF0X/LFM2.5-1.2B-Thinking-CodeX (W4A16-RTN)45.2539.7026.5669.480.76
63FlameF0X/Qwen2-0.2B-pt (W4A16-RTN)34.4025.5923.2154.410.30
65FlameF0X/Qwen2-0.2B-it (W4A16-RTN)34.2025.7423.7753.100.30
66FlameF0X/LFM2-Research (W4A16-Tuning)34.0326.3324.1851.580.06

One model placed in the top 30% of the leaderboard, two placed in the mid-field, and three smaller models placed in the 30-35% range, a band also occupied by quantized variants of OpenAI's 20B gpt-oss model.

Qwen3-4B-Distilled-Claude-4.6

The highest-ranking submission is Qwen3-4B-Distilled-Claude-4.6, a 4B-parameter multimodal model distilled from Claude Sonnet 4.6 Adaptive Thinking (the claude.ai-served variant). The objective was to assess how much of the source model's reasoning behavior could be retained at a smaller, more accessible scale.

Results:

  • NVFP4-RTN: rank 21/71, 62.68% average
  • MXFP4-RTN: rank 22/71, 61.18% average

Both quantizations place immediately behind Qwen's own Qwen3-4B-Thinking-2507 and ahead of the base Qwen3-4B MXFP4 quantization, a favorable result for a distilled variant at this size.

The model supports Transformers, vLLM, Docker, and SGLang. It is available at:

  • FlameF0X/Qwen3-4B-Distilled-Claude-4.6

1.2B LFM2.5 Variants

Two additional submissions are built on Liquid AI's non-transformer LFM2.5-1.2B architecture, which has an inherent lean toward reasoning tasks.

  • LFM2.5-1.2B-Distilled-Claude-4.6 — a distillation of Claude Sonnet 4.6 Adaptive Thinking into LiquidAI/LFM2.5-1.2B-Thinking via supervised fine-tuning. Results: 46.76% average (HellaSwag 39.51, MMLU 31.99, PiQA 68.77), rank 45/71, comparable to other approximately 1B-parameter baselines including MiniCPM5-1B and smaller Qwen3.5/2.5 variants.
  • LFM2.5-1.2B-Thinking-CodeX — the same backbone fine-tuned on 2M code examples with explicit chain-of-thought from Modotte/CodeX-2M-Thinking. Results: 45.25% average (HellaSwag 39.70, MMLU 26.56, PiQA 69.48), rank 47/71.

Both are released under Apache 2.0 and support Transformers, vLLM, and common local inference tools (llama.cpp, LM Studio, Jan, Ollama).

  • LFM2.5-1.2B-Distilled-Claude-4.6
  • LFM2.5-1.2B-Thinking-CodeX

Sub-300M Models

The remaining three submissions are considerably smaller: two 0.2B Qwen2 models and a compact LFM2-Research model trained exclusively on arXiv AI/ML text. Despite their size, their low-bit scores place them in the same range as quantized variants of OpenAI's 20B gpt-oss model.

Qwen2-0.2B-pt and Qwen2-0.2B-it

  • Qwen2-0.2B-pt — a 200M-parameter pretrained model on a mixture of Salesforce/wikitext, roneneldan/TinyStories, and Skylion007/openwebtext, without instruction tuning. Rank 63/71, 34.40% average.
  • Qwen2-0.2B-it — an instruction-tuned variant of the above, trained on a mixture of chat and reasoning datasets (HuggingFaceH4/ultrachat_200k, openai/gsm8k, microsoft/orca-math-word-problems-200k, laion/OIG, microsoft/wiki_qa). Rank 65/71, 34.20% average.

For context, OpenAI's gpt-oss-20b appears three times on the leaderboard under different quantizations, at ranks 60, 64, and 67, with averages between approximately 34% and 36%. The 0.2B models therefore occupy a similar performance band to a model roughly 100 times their size once both are quantized to 4-bit.

  • FlameF0X/Qwen2-0.2B-pt
  • FlameF0X/Qwen2-0.2B-it

LFM2-Research

LFM2-Research is a domain-specific model rather than a general-purpose assistant, trained solely on AI/ML arXiv papers (FlameF0X/arXiv-AI-ML). It uses 8 layers, a hidden size of 512, 8 attention heads with grouped query attention (4 KV heads), and a 2K context window, trained on 2,500 samples over 20 epochs. Given the narrow training distribution, some degree of overfitting should be expected.

It records a 34.03% average at rank 66/71, within the same range as gpt-oss-20b's quantized results, despite a footprint of approximately 60MB.

  • FlameF0X/LFM2-Research

Discussion

  • Distillation produces competitive results. Qwen3-4B-Distilled-Claude-4.6 performs close to Qwen's own 4B Thinking model, a reasonable outcome for a distilled variant.
  • Small models retain utility under quantization. At 4-bit precision, a well-trained 0.2B model performs comparably to a quantized 20B model on this benchmark, though the two are not interchangeable in practice.
  • Low-bit evaluation is a distinct benchmark category. For deployment on laptops, phones, or edge hardware, full-precision leaderboards do not fully characterize expected performance; this leaderboard specifically measures what survives quantization.

Planned Work

  • Further iteration on Qwen3-4B-Distilled-Claude-4.6, including more aggressive quantization and a dedicated reasoning-focused variant.
  • Modest scaling of the 0.2B Qwen2 family to determine the practical limits of this size class.
  • A second LFM2-Research training run with expanded arXiv data and a lightweight alignment stage.

All models discussed here are openly available and can be run locally. Independent benchmarking or fine-tuning results are welcome.

title: FWKV: A New Recurrent Language Model Architecture
summary: FWKV (Feed-forward Weighted Key Value) is a recurrent language model architecture designed and implemented from first principles. It replaces attention with a single multiplicative state update and shows favorable latency characteristics relative to standard Transformers.
content:
Overview

FWKV — Feed-forward Weighted Key Value — is a language model architecture developed independently of the Transformer and RWKV lineages, though it shares some conceptual ground with the latter. It is built around two specific design decisions.

Design Principles

  • A feed-forward network as the primary compute block, chosen for parallelizability and hardware efficiency.
  • A floored multiplicative recurrent state as the sole context mechanism, eliminating the need for attention or an accumulating KV cache.

The state update is defined as:

S_t = (S_{t-1} ⊙ W) + k_t ⊙ v_t

At each timestep, the previous state is decayed by a learned weight matrix W and updated by the outer product of the current key and value vectors. W is floored above zero, which prevents the model from fully forgetting prior context and yields stable, bounded memory without the complexity of explicit gating mechanisms.

Released Models

Three models are currently available under Apache 2.0:

ModelParametersTraining DataLink
FWKV-1-hf28.9MSalesforce/wikitextHF
FWKV-50M50.6MSalesforce/wikitextHF
FWKV-TinyStories—roneneldan/TinyStoriesHF

All models use the custom fwkv architecture and are compatible with Transformers and vLLM.

Latency Characteristics

Benchmarks were run on CUDA against a standard Transformer and RWKV across model sizes from 10M to 1B parameters. At 1B parameters, the Transformer baseline required approximately 82 seconds to generate 1024 tokens; RWKV required approximately 19 seconds; FWKV required approximately 39 seconds, with some variance across runs.

FWKV is currently slower than RWKV at this scale, largely due to hardware and kernel-level bottlenecks rather than a theoretical ceiling; the expectation is that further optimization would bring it closer to RWKV's range. The architecture's principal advantage at this stage is structural simplicity — no gating mechanisms and no dependency on custom CUDA kernels — making it a suitable base for further experimentation rather than a production-ready inference engine.

Resources

  • FWKV Demo on Hugging Face Spaces
  • FWKV-50M Demo
  • Full FWKV Collection

Additional model sizes and training runs are planned.

title: ChessSLM Reaches First Place on the ChessLLM Leaderboard
summary: ChessSLM, a 30M-parameter chess-playing language model, has reached first place on Maxime Labonne's ChessLLM leaderboard with a peak Elo of 1146, ahead of several larger models. This post describes the model and its current limitations.
content:
Result

ChessSLM currently holds first place on Maxime Labonne's ChessLLM leaderboard with a peak Elo of 1146, ahead of several larger models.

Model Description

ChessSLM is a specialized small language model that plays chess through autoregressive move generation in natural language, rather than board-state evaluation as in a conventional engine. It is built on a compact GPT-2 architecture of approximately 30M parameters and was pre-trained from scratch on 100,000 games from the mlabonne/chessllm dataset, formatted in Standard Algebraic Notation.

Leaderboard Standings

RankModelElo Rating
1FlameF0X/ChessSLM1146
2DedeProGames/mini-chennus1115
3EleutherAI/pythia-70m-deduped1106
4nlpguy/smolchess-v21092
5nlpguy/amdchess-v91092
6DedeProGames/dialochess1078
7FlameF0X/ChessSLM-RL1056

Performance Against Larger Models

ChessSLM demonstrates that narrowly focused training data can be effective at small scale. In head-to-head evaluation matches against larger LLM baselines, results were mixed — the model recorded a win against Claude but lost against Gemini.

Limitations

  • Illegal move generation. As a purely generative model without rules validation, it occasionally produces moves that violate the rules of chess.
  • No board-state verification. The model has no mechanism for checking legality against an internal board representation; it operates entirely on notation history as text.
  • Limited strategic depth. Performance is reasonable at lower Elo bands but does not approach dedicated engines such as Stockfish.

Planned Improvements

Future work includes move-legality filtering, board-state validation, training on larger datasets, and reinforcement learning through self-play.

ChessSLM can be played directly at the ChessLLM Space on Hugging Face.

title: Introducing LFM2.5-1.2B-Distilled-Claude-4.6 and LFM2.5-1.2B-Thinking-CodeX
summary: Two fine-tunes of Liquid AI's LFM2.5-1.2B-Thinking have been released: one distilled from Claude Sonnet 4.6's adaptive reasoning, and one trained on 2M coding examples with chain-of-thought supervision.
content:
Release Summary

Two new fine-tunes have been published to Hugging Face. This post describes each model, the motivation behind it, and how to run it.

LFM2.5-1.2B-Distilled-Claude-4.6

This model is a knowledge distillation of Claude Sonnet 4.6 Adaptive Thinking — specifically the version served on claude.ai, not the API variant — into Liquid AI's LFM2.5-1.2B-Thinking base via supervised fine-tuning. Training data was sourced from FlameF0X/Claude-Sonnet-4.6, which remains private; the resulting model is released under Apache 2.0.

The objective was to retain a meaningful portion of the source model's reasoning style and chain-of-thought behavior within a 1.2B-parameter model suitable for local deployment. The model tends to reason before producing a final answer, a behavior inherited directly from the teacher model.

The model recorded approximately 1,700 downloads within its first day of availability.

"Model trained on Claude Sonnet 4.6 Adaptive Thinking (the claude.ai variant, not the API variant). The training data is private."

LFM2.5-1.2B-Thinking-CodeX

This model takes a different approach: rather than distilling a general-purpose model, LFM2.5-1.2B-Thinking was fine-tuned on the Modotte/CodeX-2M-Thinking dataset, comprising 2 million coding examples with accompanying reasoning traces. The result is a compact model with improved performance on code generation and technical reasoning tasks.

Training was conducted using TRL's SFT trainer. The model is released under Apache 2.0 and supports standard Transformers and vLLM inference.

Benchmark Results

Results from the Intel Low-Bit Open LLM Leaderboard:

ModelAverageHellaSwagMMLUPiqaSource
LFM2.5-1.2B-Distilled-Claude-4.646.7639.5131.9968.77Intel/low bit open llm leaderboard
LFM2.5-1.2B-Thinking-CodeX45.2539.7026.5669.48Intel/low bit open llm leaderboard

Choice of Base Architecture

Liquid AI's LFM2.5 is a non-transformer sequence architecture that performs well at the 1.2B scale, with favorable speed and memory characteristics. The Thinking variant includes reasoning behavior by default, making it a suitable base for distillation work of this kind.

Availability

  • LFM2.5-1.2B-Distilled-Claude-4.6 on Hugging Face
  • LFM2.5-1.2B-Thinking-CodeX on Hugging Face

Both models are freely available and open for use.
